questions.question: The projection type used on a WAC (World Aeronautical Chart) is:
questions.explanation: WACs use a Lambert Conformal Conic projection, which is a type of conical projection.

questions.question: An example of an aviation chart using a mercator projection is a -
Rhumb lines on a VTC, appear as:
options: curved lines at lower latitudes only
questions.explanation: On conformal projections like the Lambert Conformal Conic, commonly used for VTCs, rhumb lines are represented as straight lines, which aids in navigation using constant headings.
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.3.4(b) measure rhumb line track

questions.question: What time is last light at Ayers Rock (YAYE) on the 28th of May?
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.2.2 Determine with +/- 5 min the beginning and end of civil twilight from AIP daylight and darkness graphs

questions.question: What time is first light at YMMB (Moorabbin) on 25th of November?
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.2.2 Determine with +/- 5 min the beginning and end of civil twilight from AIP daylight and darkness graphs

questions.question: What is the distance between the two paralells of longitude 143˚E and 144˚E at the equator?
questions.explanation: At the equator, the distance between two meridians (parallels of longitude) that are 1 degree apart is 60nm.
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.1.1(g) distance on the earth ie relationship between a minute of latitude and a nauitcal mile

questions.question: You are flying a leg of 67 NM with a groundspeed of 96 kt. How long will this leg take?
questions.explanation: Time = 67 NM / 96 kt ≈ 0.698 hours, 0.698 hours × 60 = ~41.9 minutes
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.1   2.4.1(a) time interval using estimated GS and distance

questions.question: One nautical mile is equal to:
questions.explanation: One nautical mile is defined as the distance covered by one minute of latitude on the Earth’s surface. This is consistent across all longitudes, making it ideal for navigation. Longitude spacing varies with latitude, but latitude spacing remains consistent — hence its use in defining the nautical mile.
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.1.1(g) distance on the earth ie relationship between a minute of latitude and a nauitcal mile

questions.question: The term 'civil twilight' is defined as
options: The period before sunrise or after sunset where the midpoint of the sun disk is 6˚ or less below the true horizon
The period before sunrise or after sunset where the midpoint of the sun disk is 6˚ or less below the apparent horizon
The period before sunrise or after sunset where the midpoint of the sun disk is 12˚ or less below the true horizon
The period before sunrise or after sunset where the midpoint of the sun disk is 12˚ or less below the apparent horizon
questions.explanation: When the Sun is between the horizon and 6° below the horizon, either after sunset or before sunrise.
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.2.5(b) period of daylight

questions.question: You plan a flight from YMMB (Moorabbin) to YSHT (Shepparton) on the 10th June with a total flight time on 2 hours 10 minutes. What is the latest ETD from YMMB (Moorabbin)?
questions.explanation:
Last light at YSHT is 0738Z 
minus 10 minutes minus 2 hours and 10 minutes = 0518Z
+1000 UTC = 1518LMT
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.2.2 Determine with +/- 5 min the beginning and end of civil twilight from AIP daylight and darkness graphs

questions.question:
Given the following latitude/longtudes: 
A= 27.00S 150.00E
B= 27.00S 135.00E
If the local mean time at position A is 1200, what is the local mean time for position B?
questions.explanation: Both positions are on the same meridan. 1 degree meridian = 4 minutes. These two positions are located 15˚ apart, therefore the time difference is 15 x 4 = 60 minutes

questions.question: Fuel at start 250L, fuel flow 48L/h, taxi allowance 7L. How much endurance will the aircraft have left after 14 minutes of flight (including reserves). Answer will ask in minutes.
You have used 46L of fuel after 50 minutes of flight, what is your average fuel consumption?
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.4.1(a) Solve G/S, distance, fuel used, fuel required, fuel remaining and fuel consumption problems, given appropriate combinations of these factors

questions.question: You are at 9,000ft and 50 DME from your destination, due to high traffic you are required to reach 1000ft above the aerodrome. The elevation of the airport is 1500ft and your ground speed is 120 knots. What is the MINIMUM rate of descent required for a continuous descent?
questions.explanation:
9000ft-1500ft-1000ft = 6500ft to lose
(50nm/120kts)*60 = 25 mins
6500ft/25 minutes = 260fpm
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.4.1(f) determine TOPC and TOPD position using average airspeed, W/V and rates of climb/descent

Last light at YSHT is 0837Z

+1000 UTC = 1728LMT

questions.explanation: ERSA - YSGE FAC

questions.question: You are at Point A at Time 0200Z enroute to Point B which is a distance of 25nm, your flight plan has you arriving at Point B at time 0215Z however, you are 2 minutes late overhead Point B. Your next point (point C) is 50nm on the same track. Recalculate your revised ETA at Point C.
questions.explanation:
Calculate G/S = 25/(17/60) = 88.23kts,
50nm/88.23kts = 0.5667 convert to minutes
= 34 minutes
12:17 + 34 minutes = 12:51

questions.question: During winter in the Southern Hemisphere, the Local Mean Time (LMT) of End of Daylight (EOD) will occur earlier at the destination than at the departure point for a flight that travels in which direction?

You are at Point A at Time 2215Z enroute to Point B which is a distance of 36nm, planning to arrive at B at time 2243Z however, you are 2 min early at B. B to C =  41nm. Recalculate your revised ETA at C.
questions.explanation:
Calculate G/S = 36/(26/60) = 83.08kts,
41nm/83.08kts = 0.4935convert to minutes
= 29.6 minutes
22:43 + 30 minutes = 23:13

questions.question: You are flying at 1,500ft, your OAT is reading 24˚C and your ground speed is 120kts. After 45 minutes, you realise that you are 3nm off track, what is your track error?
questions.explanation: 1 in 60 rule. After 60nm  of travel, you are 2nm off track

questions.question: You are flying at 3,500ft, your OAT is reading 14˚C and your ground speed is 120kts. After 30 minutes, you realise that you are 2nm off track, what is your track error?
questions.explanation: 1 in 60 rule. After 60nm  of travel, you are 2nm off track

questions.question: True Heading is 316˚ with a magnetic variation of 12˚ East, what is the magnetic heading?
questions.explanation: Remember = East is least and West is best

questions.question: True Heading is 006˚ with a magnetic variation of 12˚ East, what is the magnetic heading?

What is the expected CAS when flight planning with the following details:
Cruise Altitude: A065
OAT: 16˚C
TAS: 130kts
An aircraft flying at 8,500ft with an OAT of 17˚C and a IAS of 115kts. With 98nm remaining on its journey and a 20kt headwind, how will it take to reach to the destination?
questions.explanation:
Using the CR3, the TAS is 135kts, therefore ground speed is 115kt
98/115) x 60 = 51 minutes

questions.question: An aircraft has 72 nm to cover in the next 34 minutes in a 20 kt head wind. What CAS would be required at A085 if the temperature at cruising level is -2°C ?
questions.explanation:
First step is to work out the requied GS.
=72/(34/60) = 130.6kts 
Now to factor the 20kt wind = 130.6+20=150.6kt TAS required
using the CR3, the required CAS is 131kt
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.4.1(b) solve CAS/TAS problems given air temperature and pressure height

questions.question:
Given the following:
Flight from Point A to Point B = 247nm
Flight Fuel Available: 160L
Fuel flow: 65L/hr
What is the minimum ground speed for this flight to proceed?
questions.explanation:
160/65 = 2.46 hours of endurance
247nm/2.46 = 100.4kts

questions.question: How do radio waves travel?
How long does it take for the sun to cross to two consecutive meridians?
questions.explanation: There are 1440 minutes in a day and 360 meridans. 1440 divided by 360 = 4 minutes per meridian

questions.question: A day VFR flight should not depart if the planned ETA to its final destination is no later than:
options: 10 minutes after EOD provided runway lights are on
questions.explanation: AIP

questions.question:
Given the following:
Flight Planned Track: 216˚M
Wind: 170˚T/22kt
Magnetic Variation: 8˚E
TAS = 140kts
What is the heading to fly?
questions.explanation: The crosswind component is 17kts. At TAS of 140kt, this eqautes to a correction of 7˚

questions.question:
Given the following:
Flight Planned Track: 216˚M
Wind: 170˚T/22kt
Magnetic Variation: 8˚E
TAS = 140kts
What is the expected ground speed?
questions.explanation: The headwind component is approximately 12kts, therefore ground speed will be 140 - 12 = 128kts
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.4.1(c) determine HDG, GS and drift given TAS, W/V, TR

questions.question:
Given the following:
A = 22˚S 150˚E
B= 22.00˚S 144.00˚E
C= 25.00˚S 150.00˚E
D= 25.00˚S 144.00˚E
Comparing the distances between Point A to Point B, and Point C to Point D?
options: A to B is equal to C to D
A to B is greater than C to D
A to B is less than C to D

questions.question: Refer to the AUS PCA - You are have landed at YKAL (Kalumburu), which frequency should you cancel your SARwatch?
questions.explanation: Refer to PCA

questions.question: Refer to the AUS PCA - You are have landed at YAYE (Ayers Rock), which frequency should you cancel SARwatch?
questions.explanation: Refer to PCA

questions.question: You have departed Point A on a flight planned track of 075˚ at time 0622 with a planned ground speed of 100kts, however at 0652 are 5nm to the left of your flight planned track. The total trip from Point A to Point B is 110nm, what heading change is required to ensure arriving overhead at Point B?
questions.explanation: The Track Error is 6˚

questions.question:
Given the following:
Flight Planned Track: 047˚M
Wind: 175˚T/25kt
Magnetic Variation: 2˚E
TAS = 125kts
What is the heading to fly?
questions.explanation: The crosswind component is 20kts. At TAS of 125kt, this eqautes to a correction of 9˚
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.4.1(c) determine HDG, GS and drift given TAS, W/V, TR

questions.question:
Given the following:
Flight Planned Track: 234˚M
Wind: 308˚T/18kt
Magnetic Variation: 6˚W
TAS = 160kts
What is the correct heading to fly?
questions.explanation: The crosswind component is 17kts. At TAS of 125kt, this eqautes to a correction of 6˚. Since the crosswind is from the right, the heading will need to be increased. 234 + 9 = 243˚
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.4.1(c) determine HDG, GS and drift given TAS, W/V, TR

questions.question: With a planned fuel burn is 65L/hr, if you have started with 115L, how much fuel will you have after 47 minutes?
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.4.1(a) Solve G/S, distance, fuel used, fuel required, fuel remaining and fuel consumption problems, given appropriate combinations of these factors

questions.question: You fly a track of 225° with a TAS of 118 kt. The wind is from 140° at 30 kt. How long will it take to fly 65 NM?
questions.explanation: wind angle = 85°. Headwind ≈ 30 × cos(85°) ≈ 2.6 kt. Crosswind ≈ 30 × sin(85°) ≈ 29.8 kt. GS ≈ 115–116 kt. Time = 65 / 115 ≈ 0.565 hr = 34 minutes.

questions.question: You plan to arrive at a remote strip at 1725. Official last light is at 1728. Upon checking NOTAMs, you find the strip has no lighting. What should you do?
options: Land as planned since you’ll arrive before last light
Delay arrival until after sunset to assess lighting conditions
Divert or reschedule to ensure landing well before last light,
Proceed only if the aircraft is approved for night VFR

questions.question: First light at your departure aerodrome is at 0613 local. You plan to depart at 0610 to meet a tight schedule. What is required for the flight to be legal?
options: Departure is legal as long as civil twilight is within 10 minutes
Departure must be delayed until 0613 or later for VFR
Aircraft must have position lights turned on
Departure is legal if sunrise is before 0630
questions.explanation: VFR departure is only legal at or after first light. A 0610 departure is 3 minutes too early, even if light conditions seem adequate

questions.question: After flying 20 NM, you estimate a track error of 3°. What is your estimated displacement from track?
questions.explanation: Using the 1 in 60 rule, a 3° track error over 60nm equates to a displacement of 3nm from the intended track. At 20nm, the displacement would be 1nm.

questions.question: One nautical mile equals:
questions.explanation: A nautical mile is 1.852km and is based on the Earth's circumference and corresponds to one minute of latitude. It is the standard unit of distance used in aviation and maritime navigation.

questions.question: On a mercator chart, rhumb lines appear:
options: curved away from the nearest pole
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.3.5(a) appearance of rhumb lines, great circles, meridians and the graticule

questions.question:
Given:
Distance from A to B = 100nm
A position fix is aquired at 40nm along and you are 6nm right of track.
What is the change of heading required to arrive at Point B?
questions.explanation: Using the 1 in 60 rule, 6nm divided by 40nm x 60 = 9˚ track error. Therefore at 40nm, the track error is 9˚ with a remaining distance of 60nm. Once again using the 1 in 60 rule, a further track change of 6 will result in a displacement of 6nm from the intended track. At 40nm, the displacement would be 4nm. To correct this, the heading will need to be changed by 15˚ to the left.

questions.question: On a Lambert Conformal Conic chart, how does a rhumb line (constant true bearing) typically appear?
options: As a curved line concave to the equator
As a curved line convex to the equator
As a series of straight segments
questions.explanation: On a Lambert Conformal Conic projection, rhumb lines appear as curves concave toward the equator because the projection preserves angles but distorts shapes progressively away from standard parallels.
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.3.5(a) appearance of rhumb lines, great circles, meridians and the graticule

questions.question: How does a great circle route appear on a Lambert Conformal Conic projection?
options: As a curved line concave to the equator
As a curved line convex to the equator
As a series of straight segments
questions.explanation: The Lambert Conformal projection displays great circles as straight lines, which is why it's highly suitable for aviation route planning over mid-latitudes.
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.3.5(a) appearance of rhumb lines, great circles, meridians and the graticule

questions.question: If a pilot flies a constant true heading, how will their track appear on a Lambert Conformal chart?
options: As a curved line concave to the equator
As a curved line convex to the equator
As a series of straight segments
questions.explanation: A constant heading (rhumb line) appears as a curve concave to the equator on a Lambert chart, since only great circles appear straight on this projection.
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.3.5(a) appearance of rhumb lines, great circles, meridians and the graticule

questions.question: What would a pilot observe on the ADF indicator when approaching a thunderstorm?
options: Steady needle indication towards the NDB
No change — thunderstorms do not affect ADF
questions.explanation: Thunderstorms produce electrical discharges that can interfere with ADF signals, causing the needle to swing unpredictably.
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.6.3(c) effect of a thunderstorm

questions.question: Which condition can lead to coastal refraction errors in ADF indications?
options: Flying at high altitude over land
NDB signal crossing coastlines at an oblique angle
questions.explanation: When NDB signals cross coastlines at shallow angles, refraction can bend the signal path, causing erroneous ADF bearings — especially in coastal regions.
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.6.3(d) coastal refraction

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical ADF error?
questions.explanation: Dip error is specific to magnetic compasses, not ADFs. The other options are all recognized ADF error types.

questions.question: In which phase of flight is VOR scalloping most likely to be encountered?
options: At high altitudes over open ocean
During approach or low-altitude enroute near terrain or urban areas
Only when stationary on the ground
questions.explanation: At low altitudes, especially near hills, mountains, or cities, VOR signals are more prone to reflections, increasing the likelihood of scalloping.
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.6.5 Recall the aggregate error of a VOR and explain what is meant by scalloping

questions.question: What effect can coastal refraction have on the indications provided by an ADF in the cockpit?
options: Coastal refraction may cause the ADF pointer to eliminate all display interference in operation.
Coastal refraction may cause the ADF pointer to remain frozen on one bearing position.
Coastal refraction may cause the ADF pointer to indicate the station's bearing correctly each time.
Coastal refraction may cause the ADF pointer to show bearings off from the transmitter location.
questions.explanation: Coastal refraction bends radio waves near shorelines, which causes the ADF to display a bearing that is not the true direction to the transmitting station.
questions.kd: Unit 1.7.2   2.6.3(d) coastal refraction
